items.q: Is a basketball free throw an open or closed skill?
items.correct: Closed - the environment is stable and the shot is self-paced.
items.misconception: Open, because basketball is fast and unpredictable.
items.random_wrong: Neither - it is a fine skill only.
items.why: Classification depends on the environment, not the sport; the free throw is taken in fixed conditions.

items.q: Which practice suits a nervous beginner learning a complex skill?
items.correct: Distributed practice, with rests between attempts.
items.misconception: Massed practice, to do as many reps as possible.
items.random_wrong: No practice is needed for complex skills.
items.why: Rests prevent fatigue and overload for beginners, so distributed practice is safer and more effective.

items.q: Does extrinsic feedback come from inside the performer?
items.correct: No - extrinsic feedback comes from external sources like a coach or scoreboard.
items.misconception: Yes, it is the 'feel' of the movement.
items.random_wrong: Extrinsic and intrinsic mean the same thing.
items.why: Intrinsic feedback is the internal 'feel'; extrinsic comes from outside, so the two must not be swapped.

items.q: A tennis serve is best classified as a:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Closed skill. “Open skill” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Distributed practice is most suitable for:
items.correct: Beginners and complex or tiring skills
items.why: Correct answer: Beginners and complex or tiring skills. “Experienced performers only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
It's The performer's own senses. “The coach” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: The 'M' in SMART goals stands for:
items.why: Correct answer: Measurable. “Motivating” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
